body:
PUBLIC SAFETY COMMITTEE  
M I N U T E S  
 
Thursday, February 26, 2026, 5:00 pm  
Council Chamber, City Hall  
4949 Canada Way, Burnaby, BC  
 
PRESENT:  Councillor James Wang, Chair  
 Councillor Maita Santiago, Vice Chair  
 Councillor Richard T. Lee, Member  
 Trustee Gary Wong, Burnaby School Board Representative  
 Mike Kaerne, Burnaby Board of Trade Representative  
 Kathryn Ford, Resident Representative  
 Harvey Kooner, Resident Repres entative  
 Siqi Li, Resident Representative  
 Doris Mah, Resident Representative  
 Kam Sandhu, Resident Representative  
 Eric Yang, Resident Representative  
 Jasmine Nicholsfigueiredo , Chair District #3 - Southeast  
  
ABSENT:  Chloe Perron, Chair District  #1 - Hastings/Brentwood  
  
STAFF:  Graham de la Gorgendiere, General Manager Community Safety  
 Chief Superintendent Bill Parmar , Officer -in-Charge, Burnaby RCMP  
 Sergeant Mohammad Beyhaghi, Strategic Planning NCO, Burnaby RCMP  
 Miles Ritchie, Fire Chief, Burnaby Fire Department  
 Amy Choh, Director Engineering Transportation  
 Anastassiya Kulikov, Administrative Officer 1  
 
1. CALL TO ORDER  
The Chair called the Committee meeting to order at 5:03 pm.  
The Chair welcomed the new member to the Public Safety Committee - Resident 
Representative Doris Mah.  
 
 

Public Safety  Committee  Meeting  
Minutes  February 26, 2026  
 
 2 2. TERRITORIAL  ACKNOWLEDGEMENT  
The Chair, Councillor Wang, respectfully acknowledged that the City of Burnaby is 
located on the unceded territories of the xʷm əθkʷəy̓əm (Musqueam), S ḵwx̱wú7mesh 
(Squami sh), s əlilwətaɬ (Tsleil -Waututh), and kʷikʷ əƛ̓əm (Kwikwetlem) Peoples. Each 
Nation has distinct histories and distinct traditional territories that fully or partially 
encompass the city. The City encourages you to learn more about the Host Nations 
whose an cestors have occupied and used these lands, including parts of present -day 
Burnaby, for thousands of years.  
 
3. ADOPTION OF AGENDA  
3.1 Public Safety Committee Agenda for February 26, 2026  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT the agenda for the Public Safety Committee m eeting of Thursday, 
February 26, 2026 , be adopted, as presented.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
4. ADOPTION OF MINUTES  
4.1 Minutes of the Public Safety Committee meeting held on October 29, 2025  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT the minutes of the Public Safety Committee meet ing held on October 29, 
2025, be now adopted.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
5. DELEGATIONS AND INVITED PRESENTATIONS  
5.1 DELEGATION: Brian Cheung - Break -ins around Curle Avenue 
Neighbourhood  
Speakers:  Brian Cheung and Robert Twiddy  
Brian Cheung and Robert Twiddy appeared before the Committee expressing 
concerns with the break -ins around Curle Avenue neighbourhood.  
Brian Cheung provided details regarding recent break -in locations, community 
impact, safety concerns, increased incident severity, and absence of clear crime 
prevention strategy. The speaker  further inquired whether it is possible to 
Public Safety  Committee  Meeting  
Minutes  February 26, 2026  
 
 3 increase polic e patrolling in the area, develop a safety plan to prevent future 
crimes, and establish a community liaison so residents have a d irect line of 
communication.  
The Officer -in-Charge (OIC) , RCMP,  underlined the RCMP commitment to 
increasing safety in the area. The General Manager Community Safety echoed 
the OIC comments and emphasized the City's commitment to safety.  
 
6. ADMINISTRATIVE  REPORTS  
No Administrative Reports were received.  
 
7. INFORMATION REPORTS  
7.1 VERBAL REPORT: BURNABY RCMP COMMUNICATIONS UNIT  
Corporal Michael Kalanj, Media Relations Officer, Corporal Laura Hirst, Media 
Relations Officer, and Maria Howe, Communications Strategist, RCMP, provided 
a PowerPoint presentation regarding the work of the Burnaby RCMP 
Communications Unit.  
Cpl. Kalanj ou tlined the structure of the unit, which consists of two Media 
Relation Officers and one Community Strategist; responsibilities of the unit, 
including sharing information with the public and providing communication 
support within the detachment.  
Cpl. Kalan j further noted that external communication is the main focus of the 
unit and outlined three main categories: operational (active or unfolding 
incidents), proactive (results of proactive policy work and patrols), and 
engagement (highlighting work in commun ity). The speaker explained that 
information is mainly shared via media releases, social media, interviews and 
statements, news conferences and media availabilities, as well as annual report. 
News releases include the facts, request help from public, priva cy considerations 
are taken into consideration, and integrity of investigations is always protected.  
In conclusion, Cpl. Kalanj spoke regarding internal communications, which 
include communicating with members and municipal staff, providing 
communications support and guidance to other departments, and developing 
graphic design and printed materials.  
 
 
Public Safety  Committee  Meeting  
Minutes  February 26, 2026  
 
 4 7.2 SU: BURNABY -NEW WESTMINSTER TASK FORCE ON SEXUALLY 
EXPLOITED AND AT -RISK YOUTH (2025)  
The General Manager Community Safety and the General Manager Plannin g 
and Development submitted a report providing a status update (SU) on the 
activities of the Burnaby -New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and 
At-Risk Youth.  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT  the report titled “SU: Burnaby -New Westminster Task Force on Se xually 
Exploited and At -Risk Youth (2025)” dated February 26, 2025, be received for 
information.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
7.3 SU: RCMP STATUS REPORT (AUGUST - NOVEMBER 2025) AND ANNUAL 
PERFORMANCE PLAN QUARTERLY REPORT (JULY - SEPTEMBER 2025)  
The OIC, RCMP sub mitted a report providing a status update (SU) on policing 
operations covering August to November 2025 and the Annual Performance Plan 
(APP) quarterly report covering July to September 2025.  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT  the report titled “SU: RCMP Status Report  (August – November 2025) and 
Annual Performance Plan Quarterly Report (July – September 2025)” dated 
February 26, 2026, be received for information.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
7.4 SU: FIRE DEPARTMENT (DECEMBER 2025)  
The Fire Chief submitted a report providing t he Burnaby Fire Department's status 
update (SU) available to the end of December 2025.  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT  the report titled “SU: Fire Department Status Report” dated February 26, 
2026, be received for information.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
 
 
Public Safety  Committee  Meeting  
Minutes  February 26, 2026  
 
 5 7.5 SU: COMMUNITY SAFETY ADVISORY COMMITTEE  
Community Safety Advisory Committee (CSAC) Districts 1 and 3 submitted 
reports providing status updates on the Districts' work.  
MOVED AND SECONDED  
THAT  the Community Safety Advisory Committee (CSAC) Status Reports for 
Districts 1 and 3 be received for information.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
8. CORRESPONDENCE  
8.1 Twelfth Avenue Elementary PAC - Construction on Crosswalk of 12th Ave 
and Canada Way  
Andrea Louie submitted correspondence on behalf of Twelfth Avenue Elementary 
PAC r egarding the absence of a safe crossing at 12th Avenue and Canada Way 
near Twelfth Avenue Elementary School.  
Amy Choh, Director Engineering Transportation, advised the Committee that the 
curb has been reinstated and acknowledged the need to work collaborat ively with 
Public Works to ensure safe pedestrian access when construction is underway. 
Staff are currently reviewing available options.  
 
8.2 BC SPCA - Breed -Specific Provisions within the Animal Control Bylaw No. 
9609CC  
BC SPCA submitted correspondence r equesting that Council repeal or amend 
breed -specific legislation designating “Staffordshire Bull Terriers, American Pit 
Bull Terrier and any dog generally recognized as a pit bull or pit bull terrier and 
includes a dog of mixed breed with predominant  pit bull or pit bull terrier 
characteristics” as vicious dogs under Burnaby’s Animal Control Bylaw.   
The General Manager Community Safety, noted that while there are no current 
plans to repeal or amend the Burnaby’s Animal Control Bylaw, staff may further 
review this correspondence to determine whether amendments to the bylaw are 
warranted.  
 
9. OTHER BUSINESS  
No items of Other Business were received.  
Public Safety  Committee  Meeting  
Minutes  February 26, 2026  
 
 6  
10. ADJOURNMENT  
MOVED AND SECONED  
THAT the Public Safety Committee adjourn at 6:55 pm.  
CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY  
 
 
 
   
Councillor James Wang  
CHAIR   Anastassiya Kulikov    
ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER 1

---

None.

Purpose: To provide Committee with an overview of the Burnaby RCMP Community Safety Response Team. Staff presentation by Corporal Terry Tiganis, Acting Sergeant in Charge

---

Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on the 2025 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan. Staff presentation by Dawn Virginillo, Manager Crime Reduction and Intervention

Meeting April 23, 2026  
File: 2410 -20 
COMMITTEE  REPORT  
 
TO: PUBLIC SAFETY COMMITTEE (PSC)  
 
FROM:  GENERAL MANAGER COMMUNITY SAFETY   
  
SUBJECT:  SU: BURNABY COMMUNITY SAFETY AND WELL -BEING PLAN 
PROGRESS REPORT AND  2026 UPDATE  
 
PURPOSE : To provide Council  with a status update (SU)  on the 202 5 Community 
Safety and Well -Being Plan.  
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report titled “ SU: Burnaby Community Safety and Well -Being Plan 
Progress Report and 2026 Update ” dated April 23, 2026 , be received for 
information.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
The Community Safety and Well -Being Plan (202 5) and the Burnaby Strategic Plan 
(2022) are integral to the Community Safety and Well -Being Plan Progress Report and 
2026 Update as they provide the foundational strategic frameworks and goals for 
addressing safety, well -being, and quality of life in the community. Together, these 
strategic  documents ensure that the Burnaby Community Safety and Well -Being Plan 
Progress Report and 2026 Update  is aligned with and actively supports Burnaby's 
overall vision for a safe, inclusive, and thriving city.  
2.0 BACKGROUND  
The Community Safety and Well -Being Plan Progress Report and 2026 Update 
provides an annual overview of the initiatives and actions undertaken to enhance safety 
and well -being in the City of Burnaby. The report is grounded in an upstream approach 
to commun ity safety, recognizing well -being as fundamental to long -term crime 
reduction and prevention. It highlights key accomplishments over the past year, 
including the continued development of new programs, the strengthening of public 
safety partnerships, and t he identification of emerging issues affecting community 
safety. This work is informed by ongoing engagement with the community to ensure 
initiatives remain responsive to identified needs. The Progress Report and 2026 Update 
focuses on strengthening existi ng initiatives, addressing emerging well -being and safety 
priorities, and supporting Burnaby’s continued growth as a safe, inclusive, and resilient 
community . 
 
 
Subject:  SU: Burnaby Community Safety and Well -Being Plan Progress Report and 2026 Update  
PSC Meeting April 23, 2026  Page 2 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
Burnaby has long recognized the importance of community safety in fostering a vibrant, 
welcoming and inclusive environment. Th is commitment to safety is reflected in the 
proactive and equity ‑informed approach  that shapes this Community Safety and Well -
Being Plan Progress Report and 2026 Update . The work outlined here is designed to 
address a broad range of safety and well -being priorities  from crime prevention and 
emergency preparedness  to safer public spaces, social inclusion and access to 
supports , achieved through collaboration with residents, community organizations, and 
other safety partners .  
Central to this work is a commitment to advancing equity, addressing systemic barriers, 
and ensuring that safety initiatives reflect the diverse needs and lived experiences of 
Burnaby’s communities. Since the adoption of the 2020 Community Safety Plan , the 
City has continued to adapt and strengthen its strategies to respond to evolving 
community needs. In 202 5, numerous initiatives were successfully implemented, 
providing a strong foundation for continued progress in 202 6. Key achievements are 
highlighted b elow and further detailed in the attached report.   
3.1 Grant Opportunities for Safety Initiatives   
The C ommunity Safety Department  has taken a proactive approach to identifying and 
pursuing external grant opportunities to supplement funding for new safety initiatives  
that support the Plan’s work . These efforts strengthen the resources available to enable 
implementation of innovative and effective solutions  to expand and enhance safety 
programs  and reduce reliance on internal operating budgets . Funding applications for 
2026 and subsequent years w ould support work across various City departments, 
reflectin g the Plan’s commitment to advancing community safety through cross -
departmental collaboration.  
3.2 Data -Driven Enforcement  
The C ommunity Safety Department is continuing work  to implemen t a data -driven bylaw 
enforcement strategy to improve targeting, efficiency, and outcomes. The work will 
deliver  integrated data and performance measures to more efficiently identify priority 
concerns, guide enforcement decisions, and optimize the use of resources. This 
approach supports more responsive service delivery, measurable improvements, and 
better alignment of enforcement efforts with community needs over time.  Strengthening 
these processes supports  proactive, responsive safety planning and  helps make 
Burnaby’s streets safer for everyone . 
3.3 Cross -Departmental Safety Planning for Community Events  
The City is strengthening safety planning for large -scale community events through 
coordinated, cross -departmental collaboration. By working closely with event partners, 
Burnaby is improving risk management, enhancing public safety, and supporting 
inclusiv e, welcoming event spaces. This coordinated approach builds community trust 
and contributes to safer, vibrant community events city -wide.  
Subject:  SU: Burnaby Community Safety and Well -Being Plan Progress Report and 2026 Update  
PSC Meeting April 23, 2026  Page 3 
 
3.4 Increasing Youth Supports and Outreach  
Across City departments and the Burnaby RCMP, coordinated efforts are strengthening 
early intervention and prevention for at -risk youth. The RCMP Youth Section is 
proactively engaging young people and connecting families to timely, meaningful 
supports thro ugh partnerships with schools and community agencies, directed towards  
reducing youth involvement in criminal activity. Complementary City -led youth programs 
and services  including proposed grant -funded enhanced youth leadership opportunities 
and targeted clinical mental health supports  will further build resilience. Together, 
targeted education, early intervention, and clear pathways to services support safer 
schools, healthier decision -making, and stronger outcomes for young people.  
4.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
The Community Safety and Well -Being Plan Progress Report and 2026 Update  will be 
posted to the Community Safety web page to  make it publicly available after 
presentation to Council in Spring 202 6. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Some youth -related initiatives have been funded through securement of Public Safety 
Canada grant funding. Remaining initiatives and projects are included in respective 
departmental base budgets.  
Respectfully submitted,  
 
 
Graham de la Gorgendiere , General Manager Community Safety  
ATTACHMENTS  
Attachment 1 - Community Safety  and Well -Being Plan  Progress Report and 2026 
Update  
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS  
This report was prepared by Dawn Virginillo, Manager of Crime Reduction and 
Intervention.

---

Purpose: To provide a status update (SU) on policing operations covering December 2025 to January 2026 and the Annual Performance Plan (APP) quarterly report covering October to December 2025.

Meeting April 23, 2026  
File: 2410 -20 
COMMITTEE  REPORT  
 
TO: PUBLIC SAFETY COMMITTEE (PSC)  
 
FROM:  RCMP OFFICER IN CHARGE   
  
SUBJECT:  SU: RCMP STATUS REPORT ( DECEMBER 2025  – JANUARY  2026) 
AND ANNUAL PERFORMANCE PLAN QUARTERLY REPORT 
(OCTOBER  – DEC EMBER  2025)  
 
PURPOSE : To provide a status update (SU) on policing operations covering  
December 2025  to January  2026 and the Annual Performance Plan  
(APP) quarterly report covering October  to December  2025 . 
 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report titled “SU: RCMP Status Report ( December 2025  – January  
2026) and Annual Performance Plan Quarterly Report ( October  – December 
2025)” dated April 23, 2026, be received for information . 
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
This report aligns with the following Council -adopted policies, plans and strategies:  
Community Safety Plan (202 5) and the Burnaby Strategic Plan (2023 -26). 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND  
Burnaby RCMP detachment is represented at the Public Safety Committee by the  
Officer in Charge (OIC). The Public Safety Committee Terms of Reference outline  the 
RCMP role in the Committee which includes:  
 
- providing leadership to and support for the activities of the district Community 
Policing Advisory Committees and other volunteers; and  
- review, provide feedback and forward recommendations to Council on  a variety 
of matters related to the implementation of and citizen  involvement with 
community and problem -oriented policing.  
As a result, at each meeting of the Public Safety Committee, the OIC provides a  
detailed report covering highlights from the previous quarter.  
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
RCMP submit a report to the Public Safety Committee, which provides updates and  
highlights from various initiatives as well as traffic and crime statistics (Attachment1).   
Subject: SU: RCMP Status Report ( December 2025  – January  2026) and Annual Performance Plan 
Quarterly Report ( October  – December 2025)  
PSC Meeting April 23, 2026  Page 2 
 
In addition, a copy of the detachment’s APP quarterly report is submitted to the  Public 
Safety Committee (Attachment 2).    
4.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
This report provides information on the Burnaby RCMP Online Crime Reporting  
that was launched in 2020 and continues to be used by members of the public.  
An average of approximately 200 reports is received during each report  
period. The online reporting makes it easier for people to file certain   
non-emergency  complaints.  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Not applicable.  
Respectfully submitted,  
Sgt. Mo Beyhaghi, Strategic Planning NCO  
For 
Chief Superintendent Bill Parmar, RCMP Officer in Charge  
ATTACHMENTS  
Attachment 1  – RCMP PSC Report ( December 2025  - January  2026) 
Attachment 2  – APP Quarterly Report ( October  – December  2025)

---

Purpose: To provide the Burnaby Fire Department's status update (SU) available to the end of February 2026.

Meeting April 23, 2026  
File: 2410 -20 
COMMITTEE  REPORT  
 
TO: PUBLIC SAFETY COMMITTEE (PSC)  
 
FROM:  FIRE CHIEF   
  
SUBJECT:  SU: FIRE DEPARTMENT  (FEBRUARY 2026 ) 
 
PURPOSE : To provide the Burnaby Fire Department's status update (SU) 
available to the end of February  2026. 
RECOMMENDATION  
THAT  the report titled “SU: Fire Department (February  2026)” dated April 23, 
2026, be received for information.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION  
This report aligns with the following Council -adopted policies, plans and strategies: 
Community Safety Plan (2020) and the Burnaby Strategic Plan (2022).  
2.0 BACKGROUND  
The Burnaby Fire Department has been proudly serving the City and its citizens since 
1911. Our mission is to protect the lives and property of our citizens through the reactive 
emergency response operations of firefighting, rescue and medical aid, and the 
proactive engagements of fire prevention, inspections, code enforcement and safety 
education.  
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION  
This report is provided to outline the Burnaby Fire Department statistics and major 
events occurring in the interim period from March  2025 to the end of February  2026, for 
Public Safety Committee reference.  This report is intended to provide information and 
insight to the Public Safety Committee on Burnaby Fire Department activities.    
4.0 COMMUNICATION  AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
The Fire Department provides information and data to the community in an effort to 
maintain a high level of transparency in city services.  
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Not applicable.  
  
Subject: SU: Fire Department  (February  2026) 
PSC Meeting April 23, 2026  Page 2 
 
Respectfully submitted,  
Miles Ritchie , Fire Chief  
ATTACHMENTS  
Attachment 1  – SU - Fire Department Status Report  (February)  2026 -04-23

---

briefs.title: Council to consider Burnaby's 2026 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan update
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Public Safety Committee is set to receive a progress report on the 2025–2030 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan at its April 23, 2026 meeting. The update covers 85 initiatives across five priority areas — including neighbourhood safety, emergency preparedness, safer public spaces, homelessness response, and youth programs — and introduces 27 new or planned actions for 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review the plan's business safety section and contact crimeprevention@burnaby.ca to share input.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Public Safety Committee Minutes from February 26, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and approve the minutes from the Public Safety Committee meeting held on February 26, 2026. That meeting covered neighbourhood break-ins near Curle Avenue, a review of RCMP communications, updates on youth safety programs, fire department activity, and a request from the BC SPCA to change rules about how certain dog breeds — including pit bulls — are classified under Burnaby's Animal Control Bylaw.
briefs.action_required: If you own a dog business or property near Curle Avenue, watch for Council's response to these safety and bylaw discussions.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider administrative reports — April 23, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review administrative reports at its upcoming meeting on April 23, 2026. No specific report details or bylaw changes have been provided, so the full scope of what Council will consider is not yet known.
briefs.action_required: Check the Burnaby Council agenda for report details before the April 23, 2026 meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council administrative reports meeting date

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Overview of Burnaby RCMP Community Safety Response Team
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a verbal briefing on the Burnaby RCMP Community Safety Response Team at its upcoming meeting. Corporal Terry Tiganis, Acting Sergeant in Charge, will present an overview of how the team operates and its role in keeping the community safe. No vote is scheduled — this is an information session only.
briefs.action_required: Attend or watch the April 23, 2026 council meeting to learn how this team may affect your area.

key_dates.label: Burnaby RCMP Community Safety Response Team briefing — council meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's 2026 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan Progress Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Public Safety Committee is set to receive a progress report on the City's Community Safety and Well-Being Plan at its April 23, 2026 meeting. The report outlines what was accomplished in 2025 and what's planned for 2026 — including data-driven bylaw enforcement, better safety planning for large events, and expanded youth outreach programs funded in part by federal grants.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — new bylaw enforcement and youth safety programs could affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Safety Plan 2026 Update — public presentation to Council

briefs.title: Council to consider: Burnaby RCMP policing update and performance report for review
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Public Safety Committee is set to receive a status update from the RCMP covering policing operations from December 2025 to January 2026, along with a quarterly performance report for October to December 2025. This is an information report only — no new rules or bylaws are being proposed — but it gives the public a look at local crime trends, traffic statistics, and policing initiatives in Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: Review the RCMP report for local crime trends that may affect your business or neighbourhood.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Update for February 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby's Public Safety Committee is set to receive a status update report from the Fire Chief covering Fire Department activities from March 2025 to the end of February 2026. The report covers emergency response stats, fire prevention, inspections, and community safety education — and is being presented for information only, with no new rules or requirements proposed.

briefs.title: Council to consider Community Safety Advisory Committee update on encampments, panhandling, and e-bikes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review a status update from the Community Safety Advisory Committee (CSAC) covering concerns raised in March–April 2026. The report flags three issues affecting local businesses and residents: encampments and debris near the Willingdon overpass, increased panhandling and open drug use along Hastings Street, and safety concerns around e-bikes and e-scooters near Brentwood, Confederation Park, and local schools.
briefs.action_required: If your business is on Hastings Street, watch for any city-led crime reporting education sessions coming to your area.
briefs.industries: Retail, Grocery & Food Retail, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to Consider: No Correspondence Items on April 23, 2026 Agenda
briefs.summary: The April 23, 2026 Burnaby City Council agenda includes a correspondence section, but no items or letters have been listed under it. There is nothing for Council to review or act on in this section at this time.
